name: Drake
description: The drake, a prominent figure in European folklore and mythology, is a type of dragon, often depicted as a smaller, wingless or less powerful relative of the more imposing, classical dragon. Unlike its winged counterparts, the drake's power often resides in its cunning, strength, and potent venom. Drakes are frequently associated with hoards of treasure, guarding them fiercely within caves or underground lairs. The drake's appearance can vary, but it is commonly represented as a serpentine creature with scales, sharp claws, and a powerful tail. Its breath may be fiery or poisonous, adding to its formidable nature. While not always capable of flight, drakes are adept at navigating their terrestrial environments, making them dangerous adversaries in close combat. They symbolize greed, territoriality, and the perils of unchecked ambition, often serving as obstacles in heroic quests and cautionary tales.
subtitle: A wingless or lesser dragon of European folklore.
appearance: Serpentine body covered in scales, sharp claws, powerful tail, and a draconic head with fangs.
habitat: Caves, underground lairs, forests, and mountainous regions.
behavior: Territorial, greedy, cunning, and fiercely protective of its hoard.
weaknesses: Vulnerability to certain types of magic

keyMyths.title: The Dragon of Wantley
keyMyths.description: This English ballad tells the story of More of More Hall, who slays a dragon (likely a drake) terrorizing the village of Wantley. The dragon is described as having a sting and causing much devastation before More defeats it with his cunning and strength.
keyMyths.source: Percy's Reliques of Ancient English Poetry

keyMyths.title: Fafnir's Transformation
keyMyths.description: While Fafnir eventually becomes a full-fledged dragon, his initial transformation in Norse mythology can be seen as an evolution into a drake-like form. Driven by greed for Andvari's gold, Fafnir slays his father and takes the treasure, gradually transforming into a monstrous, dragon-like creature, guarding his hoard.

keyMyths.title: The Lambton Worm
keyMyths.description: This folktale from County Durham, England, tells of a monstrous worm (often interpreted as a large drake) that terrorizes the countryside. The worm is eventually defeated by Sir John Lambton, who wears spiked armor to prevent the worm from crushing him to death.

sources.author: Jorge Luis Borges
sources.description: A compendium of mythical creatures, including various types of dragons and drakes.
sources.text: A compendium of mythical creatures, including various types of dragons and drakes.

sources.author: Carol Rose
sources.work: Giants, Dragons, and Unicorns: Myths and Legends of the Middle Ages
sources.description: Explores the symbolism and cultural significance of dragons and related creatures in medieval literature and art.
sources.text: Explores the symbolism and cultural significance of dragons and related creatures in medieval literature and art.

sources.author: Richard Barber
sources.description: A survey of the medieval bestiary tradition, which often includes descriptions of dragons and their characteristics.
sources.text: A survey of the medieval bestiary tradition, which often includes descriptions of dragons and their characteristics.

sources.author: J.R.R. Tolkien
sources.description: While focusing on a dragon, Tolkien's analysis provides insight into the archetypal dragon figure in European literature, including the drake's attributes.
sources.text: While focusing on a dragon, Tolkien's analysis provides insight into the archetypal dragon figure in European literature, including the drake's attributes.
